original_abstract: Abstract Many peptide hormones form an α-helix on binding their receptors 1–4 , and sensitive methods for their detection could contribute to better clinical management of disease 5 . De novo protein design can now generate binders with high affinity and specificity to structured proteins 6,7 . However, the design of interactions between proteins and short peptides with helical propensity is an unmet challenge. Here we describe parametric generation and deep learning-based methods for designing proteins to address this challenge. We show that by extending RFdiffusion 8 to enable binder design to flexible targets, and to refining input structure models by successive noising and denoising (partial diffusion), picomolar-affinity binders can be generated to helical peptide targets by either refining designs generated with other methods, or completely de novo starting from random noise distributions without any subsequent experimental optimization. The RFdiffusion designs enable the enrichment and subsequent detection of parathyroid hormone and glucagon by mass spectrometry, and the construction of bioluminescence-based protein biosensors. The ability to design binders to conformationally variable targets, and to optimize by partial diffusion both natural and designed proteins, should be broadly useful.
humanized_abstract: Intracellular peptide hormone receptors bind their ligands primarily as α-helices 1–4 and thus sensitive detection methods for peptide hormones would be valuable for clinical applications of a number of endocrine diseases 5 . De novo protein design has demonstrated the feasibility of designing binders to large, structured proteins with high affinity and specificity 6,7 . However, designing high affinity interactions between proteins and short sequences of peptides that are intrinsically helical is a challenge that remains to be addressed. Here, we describe a parametric approach and a suite of deep learning tools for designing such interactions. We apply an extension of the RFdiffusion method 8 to design a binder to flexible targets, and apply a method of partial diffusion by iteratively noising and denoising the input structures to generate picomolar affinity binders to short helical peptides. These can be generated from either optimized designs derived from other design approaches or from scratch from a random noise distribution of no experimental value that requires adjustment. Designs generated with our method enable the enrichment and detection of parathyroid hormone and glucagon by mass spectrometry, as well as the construction of bioluminescent protein biosensors. Our findings for designing binders to flexible targets and optimizing both natural and designed proteins by a process of partial diffusion should be widely applicable.
